title: Apply: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965
description: Apply your understanding of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965 with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Elections and Voting Rights: From the Founding through the Civil Rights Movement
The Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965
Apply: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from President John F. Kennedy’s 1963 "Report to the American People on Civil Rights."**

The heart of the question is whether all Americans are to be afforded equal rights and equal opportunities, whether we are going to treat our fellow Americans as we want to be treated. If an American, because his skin is dark, cannot eat lunch in a restaurant open to the public, if he cannot send his children to the best public school available, if he cannot vote for the public officials who represent him, if, in short, he cannot enjoy the full and free life which all of us want, then who among us would be content to have the color of his skin changed and stand in his place? Who among us would then be content with the [[☃ definition 1]] of patience and delay?

Next week I shall ask the Congress of the United States to act, to make a commitment it has not fully made in this century to the proposition that race has no place in American life or law.

**Which statement best summarizes Kennedy’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Congress has made great strides in addressing racial segregation across the nation through key legislation.
Congress needs to address racial inequality, as not all Americans get the same rights and opportunities.
Congress must appeal to the states to end racial discrimination and segregation because national legislation has not worked.
Congress must pass an amendment to guarantee equal rights and protections for all people, regardless of race.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from President John F. Kennedy’s 1963 "Report to the American People on Civil Rights."**

The heart of the question is whether all Americans are to be afforded equal rights and equal opportunities, whether we are going to treat our fellow Americans as we want to be treated. If an American, because his skin is dark, cannot eat lunch in a restaurant open to the public, if he cannot send his children to the best public school available, if he cannot vote for the public officials who represent him, if, in short, he cannot enjoy the full and free life which all of us want, then who among us would be content to have the color of his skin changed and stand in his place? Who among us would then be content with the [[☃ definition 1]] of patience and delay?

Next week I shall ask the Congress of the United States to act, to make a commitment it has not fully made in this century to the proposition that race has no place in American life or law.

**What law is meant to directly address the many problems Kennedy highlights in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Civil Rights Act of 1964
The Twenty-Fourth Amendment
The Voting Rights Act of 1965
The Twenty-Fifth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s opinion in *South Carolina v. Katzenbach* (1966).**

Congress exercised its authority under the Fifteenth Amendment in an inventive manner when it enacted the Voting Rights Act of 1965. First: the measure [[☃ definition 1]] remedies for voting discrimination which go into effect without any need for prior [[☃ definition 2]]. This was clearly a legitimate response to the problem, for which there is ample precedent under other constitutional provisions. . . . Congress had found that case-by-case [[☃ definition 3]] was inadequate to combat widespread and persistent discrimination in voting, because of the [[☃ definition 4]] amount of time and energy required to overcome the [[☃ definition 5]] tactics invariably encountered in these lawsuits. After enduring nearly a century of systematic resistance to the Fifteenth Amendment, Congress might well decide to shift the advantage of time and [[☃ definition 6]] from the perpetrators of the evil to its victims. . . .

**Which statement best summarizes the Court’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the process of making a formal legal decision in a court
the process of taking cases to court and conducting trials

choices.content: The Voting Rights Act was constitutional when passed, but the original issues of discrimination have been addressed and are no longer relevant.
The Voting Rights Act is unconstitutional and extends congressional power beyond the power granted in the Constitution.
The Voting Rights Act is constitutional under the Fifteenth Amendment, as relying on individual court cases to enforce voting rights is not efficient or effective.
The Voting Rights Act is unconstitutional, as any voting rights issues need to be addressed within the court system.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s opinion in *South Carolina v. Katzenbach* (1966).**

Congress exercised its authority under the Fifteenth Amendment in an inventive manner when it enacted the Voting Rights Act of 1965. First: the measure [[☃ definition 1]] remedies for voting discrimination which go into effect without any need for prior [[☃ definition 2]]. This was clearly a legitimate response to the problem, for which there is ample precedent under other constitutional provisions. . . . Congress had found that case-by-case [[☃ definition 3]] was inadequate to combat widespread and persistent discrimination in voting, because of the [[☃ definition 4]] amount of time and energy required to overcome the [[☃ definition 5]] tactics invariably encountered in these lawsuits. After enduring nearly a century of systematic resistance to the Fifteenth Amendment, Congress might well decide to shift the advantage of time and [[☃ definition 6]] from the perpetrators of the evil to its victims. . . .

**What was the impact of the decision in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the process of making a formal legal decision in a court
the process of taking cases to court and conducting trials

choices.content: It upheld the Voting Rights Act as constitutional.
It struck down the Voting Rights Act as unconstitutional.
It expanded states’ powers to regulate elections.
It limited the power of Congress to implement the Voting Rights Act.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Shelby County v. Holder* (2013).**

In 1965, the States could be divided into two groups: those with a recent history of voting tests and low voter registration and turnout, and those without those characteristics. Congress based its coverage formula on that distinction. Today the Nation is no longer divided along those lines, yet the Voting Rights Act continues to treat it as if it were. . . .

Our country has changed, and while any racial discrimination in voting is too much, Congress must ensure that the legislation it passes to remedy that problem speaks to current conditions.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Voting Rights Act was always an unconstitutional extension of congressional power and needs to be struck down.
Congress can use any method necessary to address issues related to racial discrimination in elections.
The Voting Rights Act was necessary to address racial discrimination, and since racial discrimination still exists, it is still necessary.
Congress needs to update parts of the Voting Rights Act that do not reflect current conditions and unfairly target certain states.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Shelby County v. Holder* (2013).**

In 1965, the States could be divided into two groups: those with a recent history of voting tests and low voter registration and turnout, and those without those characteristics. Congress based its coverage formula on that distinction. Today the Nation is no longer divided along those lines, yet the Voting Rights Act continues to treat it as if it were. . . .

Our country has changed, and while any racial discrimination in voting is too much, Congress must ensure that the legislation it passes to remedy that problem speaks to current conditions.

**What was the impact of the decision in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It struck down key provisions of the Voting Rights Act.
It ruled that the entire Voting Rights Act was unconstitutional.
It upheld the entire Voting Rights Act as constitutional.
It expanded Congress’s powers under the Voting Rights Act.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Civil Rights Commission’s 1959 Report on Voting.**

Legislation presently on the books is [[☃ definition 1]] to assure that all our qualified citizens shall enjoy the right to vote. There exists here a striking gap between our principles and our everyday practices. There is a moral gap . . . It runs counter to our traditional concepts of fair play. It is a partial [[☃ definition 2]] of our faith in the democratic system. It undermines the moral [[☃ definition 3]] of our national [[☃ definition 4]] in international affairs.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: not sufficient to meet a need

choices.content: Voting rights laws are not strict enough, as they allow people without the proper qualifications to vote in important elections.
Democracy requires that all citizens have the right to vote, and the United States needs to spread democratic principles to other countries.
Recent legislative measures have made it easier for all people to vote regardless of race or gender, upholding American democracy.
Current voting rights laws do not ensure that every person can vote, which goes against American values.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Civil Rights Commission’s 1959 Report on Voting.**

Legislation presently on the books is [[☃ definition 1]] to assure that all our qualified citizens shall enjoy the right to vote. There exists here a striking gap between our principles and our everyday practices. There is a moral gap . . . It runs counter to our traditional concepts of fair play. It is a partial [[☃ definition 2]] of our faith in the democratic system. It undermines the moral [[☃ definition 3]] of our national [[☃ definition 4]] in international affairs.

**Which statement best describes the “striking gap” the author references in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: not sufficient to meet a need

choices.content: The federal government banned the use of poll taxes, but states still used literacy tests to deny people the right to vote.
The federal government passed laws eliminating barriers to voting, but states refused to enforce these laws in local and state elections.
The federal government gave Black people the right to vote, but Black Americans still faced significant barriers to voting.
The federal government refused to enforce the Fifteenth and Nineteenth Amendments, effectively denying Black people the right to vote.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Frederic Douglas Lewis’s testimony before the U.S. Commission on Civil Rights in 1960. Lewis has just been asked, “Why do you want to register and vote?”**  

[[☃ explanation 1]]


First, I want to be a full-fledged citizen, and we are taxpayers; we pay our taxes; we have no representation. You take for an example, if some of us happen to get into trouble with the law, when we are arrested, we are arrested by a white policeman, and are [[☃ definition 1]] by a white grand jury, and we are tried before a [[☃ definition 2]] or trial jury. . . .

So, you see, we have nobody to represent us, on the jury, school board office, the State legislature, nowhere. All the laws are being passed we have no voice in, whether it is for us or against us, and I don’t think you can find many that is for us. 

**Which statement best summarizes Lewis’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.explanation: U.S. Commission on Civil Rights, “Tuesday Morning Session Hearing: September 27, 1960,” [Commission on Civil Rights,](https://www.usccr.gov/files/historical/1960/P-019.pdf) 1960.

choices.content: The Civil Rights Commission should not focus on voting rights because there are more pressing issues for them to address.
Voting is essential for equal participation in society, ensuring fair treatment within the legal system and a voice in government decisions.
Even if Black people are given the right to vote, they will still not enjoy all of the privileges of citizenship.
The right to vote will only be worthwhile if there are more Black representatives in state legislatures and Congress.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Civil Rights Commission’s 1961 Report on Voting.**

[T]he new Federal laws concerned with discriminatory denials of the right to vote have been [[☃ definition 1]] and effectively [[☃ definition 2]]. But [[☃ definition 3]] is necessarily a long, hard, and expensive process, affecting one county at a time; and much remains to be done before the right to vote is secure against discrimination in every part of the nation. . . . 

So in 1961 the [[☃ definition 4]] is denied entirely by some because of race and [[☃ definition 5]] for many others. The promise of the Constitution is not yet fulfilled. 

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: in an energetic and forceful manner
a legal proceeding in a court

choices.content: The federal government has passed several laws that allow states to deny people the right to vote based on race, violating constitutional principles.
State and local courts are supporting  federal efforts to ban racial discrimination in voting, ensuring that all Americans now have voting rights.
Federal efforts to eliminate racial discrimination have been ineffective, as counties follow the laws only after the courts order them to do so.
The federal government has passed radical and effective measures to ensure that all people have the right to vote regardless of race.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This data is from a report from the U.S. Commission on Civil Rights, Participation 1968.**

|| **Nonwhite Voter Registration in Selected Southern States** ||
State | 1964 | 1967
- | - | -
Alabama | 19.3\% | 51.6\%
Arkansas | 40.4\% | 62.8\%
Florida | 51.2\% | 63.6\%
Georgia | 27.4\% | 52.6\%
Louisiana | 31.6\% | 58.9\%
Mississippi| 6.7\% | 59.8\%
North Carolina | 46.8\% | 51.3\%
South Carolina | 37.3\% | 51.2\%
Tennessee | 69.5\% | 71.7\%
Texas | 53.1\% | 61.6\%
Virginia | 38.3\% | 55.6\%

**Which statement best describes the trend shown in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Fewer nonwhite voters participated in elections in 1967.
More nonwhite voters participated in elections in 1967.
Fewer nonwhite voters registered to vote in 1967.
More nonwhite voters registered to vote in 1967.

**Which piece of legislation most directly contributed to the change depicted in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Voting Rights Act of 1965
The National Voter Registration Act of 1993
The Nineteenth Amendment
The Twenty-Sixth Amendment
